text.title: Outcome Roadmap Structure
text.summary: The anatomy of an outcome-based roadmap: outcome block format, contrast with feature roadmaps, and quarterly template structure.

section.title: Feature vs Outcome Roadmap
section.p: A feature roadmap commits to solutions: "Build chat feature", "Add dark mode", "Launch mobile app". An outcome roadmap commits to metric movements: "Reduce support tickets by 30%", "Improve evening retention", "Enable on-the-go usage". The solution list under each outcome is marked "to-validate" and is mutable mid-quarter.

rule.statement: Every outcome must follow the pattern: verb + metric + delta + timeframe. Example: "Reduce churn from 8% to 5% by end of Q2." Outcomes missing any component are invalid.
rule.rationale: The four-part pattern makes outcomes falsifiable. "Improve retention" cannot be scored at quarter-end; "Increase Day-30 retention from 20% to 35% by EOQ2" can.

rule.statement: Each quarterly outcome block must contain: outcome statement (verb+metric+delta+timeframe), metric with baseline, target, evidence (data source or research), candidate solutions (3–6, all marked "to-validate"), and an explicit not-doing list (2–3 items).
rule.rationale: The not-doing list is as important as the candidate solutions. It stops scope creep, sets stakeholder expectations, and creates a record for future retrospectives.

section.title: Quarterly Structure
section.p: Q1 slot holds the outcome + experiments to run + metrics to track + potential solutions (not committed) + success criteria. Q2 slot follows the same structure. Each quarter is a frozen outcome with a mutable solution list. Outcomes are set at quarter boundaries; the solution list mutates as experiments resolve.

rule.statement: Cap quarterly outcomes at 3 for solo/small team, 5 for a team with multiple streams. More outcomes destroy focus; agents will pad to fill any number you allow.
rule.rationale: Focus is the primary mechanism. Every additional outcome dilutes the attention and resources available for the others. Three well-executed outcomes beat five half-pursued ones.
